source.title: Infants' response to entrapment between contradictory messages in face-to-face interaction
source.venue: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

confidence_rationale: The foundational still-face paradigm demonstrates that infants as young as 3-4 months immediately detect when a caregiver's interactive behavior violates the expected contingency structure of face-to-face play. Infants respond with reduced positive affect, social bidding, and eventual withdrawal -- not just at play boundaries but during the unresponsiveness itself. This is classic evidence that continuous monitoring (not just boundary detection) is operational from early infancy. The original paper has been replicated hundreds of times across cultures. High source quality. Mapping fidelity is high: the paradigm directly tests whether infants track within-episode contingency, not just episode transitions.
failure_signatures: The SFP disrupts adult behavior entirely (not just content changes mid-game), so it does not isolate monitoring from detection of partner state change.
Cannot distinguish whether infants monitor frame state continuously or only sample it at high frequency.
Still-face collapses contingency and affect -- the monitoring signal and the affective channel are conflated.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Three-to-four-month-old infants immediately show distress, social bidding, and withdrawal when a caregiver adopts a neutral still face mid-interaction, violating the normal contingency between infant bids and maternal response. The effect includes a partial carry-over into the reunion episode (residual negative affect after normal interaction resumes).
mapping.ree_translation: This is the earliest and most replicated evidence that continuous frame monitoring is a primitive capacity. The infant is not merely detecting play start/stop boundaries; it monitors within-episode contingency in near-real-time and generates a violation signal when expected contingent responding fails. For REE: the play frame tag (ARC-049) requires an ongoing heartbeat signal, not just open/close bookends, even in the simplest sensorimotor play. Q-035 resolves toward: continuous monitoring is present from ~3 months; what escalates across play types is the content being monitored (simple contingency -> rules -> strategies), not the monitoring architecture.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The SFP uses complete maternal unresponsiveness as the manipulation -- a total contingency collapse, not a subtle content change. It may overestimate the sensitivity of the monitoring signal since partial contingency perturbations (as in normal play variation) produce smaller effects.
mapping.source_context: Original SFP: mother-infant dyads, infants 3-6 months, laboratory face-to-face interaction. Replicated in 80+ studies (Mesman et al. 2009 meta-analysis).

confidence_components.notes: Foundational seminal paper with massive replication record across cultures (including non-WEIRD samples). Source quality highest possible for a behavioral paradigm. Transfer risk low: monitoring behavior is pre-linguistic, pre-theory-of-mind, primitive -- directly relevant to REE sensorimotor and early play substrates.
